title: Salary Disparities Across Disciplines
text: This project examines salary disparities across disciplines and explores who benefits most from the rise of artificial intelligence.

title: Salary Disparities Across Disciplines
text:
Research Question
Who benefits most from AI’s rise?

title: Salary Disparities Across Disciplines
text:
Team Members

Natalie Baclawski
Haydn Thomas
Kelly Xiao

title: Literature Review
text: Artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a force shaping modern labor markets in real-time. The expansion of generative AI technologies has accelerated changes in how work is performed, which skills are valued, and how compensation is distributed across occupations. Our research suggests that AI does not affect all workers equally. Instead, its impact varies significantly depending on the nature of job tasks, the level of worker skill, and the degree to which occupations can integrate AI. As a result, AI has the potential to both increase productivity and reshape wage distributions across disciplines. Understanding these dynamics is critical for evaluating how AI may influence salary disparities across industries and geographic regions. Recent academic literature highlights that the relationship between AI and wages is complex and evolving. Georgieff (2024) finds that earlier forms of AI did not significantly increase wage inequality between occupations, although this evidence predates the widespread adoption of generative AI. Other studies suggest that the current wave of AI adoption may have stronger and more differentiated effects across the labor market. For example, Hartley et al. (2024) shows that gen AI usage is more prevalent among higher-income, more educated workers, indicating that those already positioned in knowledge-intensive roles may be more likely to benefit from AI adoption. At the same time, Brynjolfsson et al. (2025) provides evidence that AI can significantly increase worker productivity - particularly among less experienced employees - and suggests that AI may also help narrow performance gaps within certain occupations. Further research emphasizes that AI’s impact extends beyond wages. Engberg et al. (2025) find that AI exposure is positively associated with wages at the worker level, but that these effects vary by occupation and skill composition. Meanwhile, Frank et al. (2025) argue that traditional labor market indicators such as wages and employment levels may not fully capture AI-driven disruption, as some occupations face increasing instability even if wages remain stable. The literature suggests that AI is likely to amplify differences between workers who can effectively leverage the technology and those whose tasks are more easily automated. This makes it important to examine salary disparities across disciplines, as well as differences between AI-related and non AI-related roles, geographic regions, and work arrangements such as remote versus in-person employment. This study seeks to analyze how AI is influencing salary outcomes across occupations and industries, with a focus on identifying which groups benefit most from its adoption and whether existing inequalities are being reinforced or reduced.
